ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Vietnamese-style shredded chicken and cabbage salad (Goi Bap Cai Ga Xe). FOODON_00002809 (chicken salad food product) is used as the closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as closest parent for shredded chicken in salad context
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Vietnamese shredded chicken and cabbage salad. A00LC (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Vietnamese salad dish.

allergens: gluten (rice crackers may contain trace gluten)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, representing a full plate of shredded cabbage and chicken breast salad with dressing and a side of mini rice crackers, as typically served at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken breast, cooked, roasted (NDB 05064) and Raw cabbage (NDB 11109), combined with Vietnamese salad dressing estimation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Goi Bap Cai Ga Xe exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked chicken breast (SR Legacy NDB 05064), raw green cabbage (NDB 11109), white onion (NDB 11282), and a Vietnamese sweet-sour dressing (lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, chilli) modelled from USDA component data. Rice cracker contribution is included at approximately 10% by weight. Weighted composite per 100 g of the assembled dish is reported. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; cabbage and mixed vegetable salad with lean protein analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Goi Bap Cai Ga Xe. GI of 45 is estimated by analogy with mixed vegetable salads with lean protein and a sweet-sour dressing containing moderate sugar. Cabbage has a very low GI (~10); the dressing sugar and rice crackers elevate the composite GI. The estimate of 45 (low category) is consistent with similar Vietnamese salad dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (45 × 27.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 12.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and dressing
hypertension.guidance: Each 300 g serving provides approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or dressing on the side to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of 12.4 per serving, this salad is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without additional rice or high-sugar condiments, as the high protein and fibre content from cabbage help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein, low saturated fat, and vegetable-rich composition
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides lean chicken breast with low saturated fat (approximately 1.5 g per serving) and is rich in cabbage, which supplies vitamin C and fibre; the primary cardiovascular concern is the sodium from fish sauce-based dressing, which should be moderated for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Goi Bap Cai Ga Xe (Gỏi Bắp Cải Gà Xé) is a Vietnamese-style shredded cabbage and chicken breast salad that is popular in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore, including at Co Chung Restaurant. The dish consists of finely shredded fresh green cabbage and white onion tossed with hand-pulled or shredded poached chicken breast, dressed in a Vietnamese sweet-sour dressing typically made from lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, garlic, and fresh chilli. It is served with mini rice crackers on the side for added crunch. The texture is crisp and refreshing, with a balance of sweet, sour, savoury, and mildly spicy flavours characteristic of Vietnamese goi (salad) cuisine. Nutritionally, the dish is relatively low in calories (approximately 98 kcal per 100 g) and provides a good source of lean protein from chicken breast (approximately 9.8 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrates from the dressing sugar and rice crackers, and dietary fibre from the cabbage. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low GI), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The main clinical consideration is the sodium content from fish sauce, which may be relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and halal-suitable. It is commonly consumed as a starter or light main course and is well suited to weight management and low-calorie dietary patterns.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Goi Bap Cai Ga Xe was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked chicken breast, raw cabbage, onion, and a modelled Vietnamese sweet-sour dressing. GI is an analogous estimate based on similar mixed vegetable and lean protein salads from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (SR Legacy: cooked chicken breast NDB 05064, raw cabbage NDB 11109, white onion NDB 11282) combined with a modelled Vietnamese sweet-sour dressing and approximately 10% rice cracker contribution by weight. No exact match was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) per USDA FoodData Central data for the component ingredients; chicken breast and cabbage contain negligible trans fat. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this specific dish does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 5. Sodium content is estimated and may vary significantly depending on the quantity of fish sauce used by the restaurant. 6. The dish is served with mini rice crackers which contribute to carbohydrate and sodium content; the nutrient values reflect the composite dish including crackers at approximately 10% by weight. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the ingredients (chicken, vegetables, lime, fish sauce) but has not been independently verified for Co Chung Restaurant. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and carry medium confidence.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Singaporean cuisine requirement (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Vietnamese as dish origin language.
Estimated nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for cooked chicken breast, raw cabbage, onion, and modelled Vietnamese dressing with rice cracker contribution.
Assigned GI of 45 (low) by analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables and University of Sydney GI Database for similar mixed vegetable and lean protein salads.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg as -1 with explicit note explaining it is not reported in the source.
Documented trans_fat_g as 0.0 confirmed zero per USDA component data.
